what: 9. Top 10 districts serve 15% of all students
title: New Jersey Enrollment Insights
text: Just 10 nearly 580 districts educate 1 7 NJ students. Newark alone 43,000.
code: top_10 <- enr_current %>%   filter(is_district, !is_charter,          subgroup == "total_enrollment", grade_level == "TOTAL") %>%   arrange(desc(n_students)) %>%   head(10) %>%   mutate(district_label = reorder(district_name, n_students))  stopifnot(nrow(top_10) > 0) top_10 %>% select(district_name, n_students) #>                               district_name n_students #> 1             Newark Public School District    43216.0 #> 2                  Elizabeth Public Schools    26708.0 #> 3                Jersey City Public Schools    25307.0 #> 4           Paterson Public School District    21849.0 #> 5           Edison Township School District    16191.0 #> 6            Trenton Public School District    14379.5 #> 7       Toms River Regional School District    13925.0 #> 8       Woodbridge Township School District    13425.0 #> 9  Hamilton Township Public School District    12112.0 #> 10               Union City School District    12006.0  ggplot(top_10, aes(x = district_label, y = n_students)) +   geom_col(fill = nj_colors["total"]) +   coord_flip() +   scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +   labs(title = "Top 10 Districts Serve 15% of All Students",        subtitle = "Just 10 out of nearly 580 districts educate about 1 in 7 NJ students",        x = "", y = "Students") +   theme_nj()

title: New Jersey Enrollment Insights
text: Multiracial students grew 46% since 2020 - 2.4% 3.5% enrollment - making fastest-growing racial category NJ.
code: multi <- state_summary %>%   filter(subgroup == "multiracial", grade_level == "TOTAL")  stopifnot(nrow(multi) > 0) multi %>% select(end_year, n_students, pct) #> # A tibble: 7 × 3 #>   end_year n_students    pct #>      <dbl>      <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1     2020     32622  0.0237 #> 2     2021     34518  0.0253 #> 3     2022     37474  0.0275 #> 4     2023     40934. 0.0298 #> 5     2024     43436. 0.0315 #> 6     2025     45246. 0.0328 #> 7     2026     47160  0.0347  ggplot(multi, aes(x = end_year, y = pct * 100)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1.5, color = nj_colors["multiracial"]) +   geom_point(size = 3, color = nj_colors["multiracial"]) +   labs(title = "Multiracial Students: Fastest-Growing Category",        subtitle = "From 2.4% to 3.5% of enrollment since 2020 (46% growth)",        x = "School Year", y = "Percent Multiracial") +   theme_nj()

title: New Jersey Enrollment Insights
text: NJ’s Pre-K enrollment surged 45,000 88,000 since 2020 - nearly doubling, fueled state’s expanding universal pre-K program.
code: prek <- state_summary %>%   filter(subgroup == "total_enrollment", grade_level == "PK")  stopifnot(nrow(prek) > 0) prek %>% select(end_year, n_students) #> # A tibble: 7 × 2 #>   end_year n_students #>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1     2020      45013 #> 2     2021      56396 #> 3     2022      65350 #> 4     2023      71615 #> 5     2024      83463 #> 6     2025      87231 #> 7     2026      88063  ggplot(prek, aes(x = end_year, y = n_students)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1.5, color = nj_colors["prek"]) +   geom_point(size = 3, color = nj_colors["prek"]) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, limits = c(0, NA)) +   labs(title = "Pre-K Nearly Doubled Since 2020",        subtitle = "From 45,000 to 88,000 students - NJ's universal pre-K expansion",        x = "School Year", y = "Pre-K Students") +   theme_nj()

what: 12. Bergen County has more students than several US states
title: New Jersey Enrollment Insights
text: 130,000+ students, Bergen County alone larger public school system entire states like Wyoming, Vermont, North Dakota.
code: county_enr <- enr_current %>%   filter(is_district, subgroup == "total_enrollment", grade_level == "TOTAL") %>%   group_by(county_name) %>%   summarize(n_students = sum(n_students, na.rm = TRUE),             n_districts = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%   filter(county_name != "Charters") %>%   arrange(desc(n_students)) %>%   head(15) %>%   mutate(county_label = reorder(county_name, n_students))  stopifnot(nrow(county_enr) > 0) county_enr #> # A tibble: 15 × 4 #>    county_name n_students n_districts county_label #>    <chr>            <dbl>       <int> <fct>        #>  1 Bergen         130172.          76 Bergen       #>  2 Essex          125813           23 Essex        #>  3 Middlesex      121810.          25 Middlesex    #>  4 Union           95696.          23 Union        #>  5 Monmouth        87674           51 Monmouth     #>  6 Hudson          80614           13 Hudson       #>  7 Camden          77633           39 Camden       #>  8 Passaic         73151           20 Passaic      #>  9 Morris          72082           40 Morris       #> 10 Burlington      68970           39 Burlington   #> 11 Ocean           63832.          28 Ocean        #> 12 Mercer          58960           12 Mercer       #> 13 Somerset        48696.          19 Somerset     #> 14 Gloucester      46076.          28 Gloucester   #> 15 Atlantic        40586           24 Atlantic  ggplot(county_enr, aes(x = county_label, y = n_students)) +   geom_col(fill = nj_colors["total"]) +   coord_flip() +   scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +   labs(title = "Bergen County Has More Students Than Several US States",        subtitle = "Top 15 NJ counties by enrollment",        x = "", y = "Students") +   theme_nj()

title: New Jersey Enrollment Insights
text: Half NJ’s 580 districts fewer 1,200 students. median district smaller single large high school.
code:
dist_sizes <- enr_current %>%   filter(is_district, !is_charter,          subgroup == "total_enrollment", grade_level == "TOTAL")  stopifnot(nrow(dist_sizes) > 0) cat("Districts:", nrow(dist_sizes), "
") #> Districts: 579 cat("Median:", median(dist_sizes$n_students, na.rm = TRUE), "
") #> Median: 1162 cat("Under 1000:", sum(dist_sizes$n_students < 1000, na.rm = TRUE), "
") #> Under 1000: 266 cat("Over 10000:", sum(dist_sizes$n_students > 10000, na.rm = TRUE), "
") #> Over 10000: 14  ggplot(dist_sizes, aes(x = n_students)) +   geom_histogram(binwidth = 500, fill = nj_colors["total"], color = "white") +   geom_vline(xintercept = median(dist_sizes$n_students, na.rm = TRUE),              linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 1) +   scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +   labs(title = "Most NJ Districts Are Tiny",        subtitle = "Half have fewer than 1,200 students (red line = median)",        x = "Students", y = "Number of Districts") +   theme_nj()

what: 14. NJ’s enrollment pyramid shows the pre-K boom
title: New Jersey Enrollment Insights
text: 2026 grade-level distribution reveals pre-K surge: PK enrollment (88K) now surpassed Kindergarten (87K), reflecting NJ’s universal pre-K push.
code: grade_enr <- state_summary %>%   filter(end_year == 2026, subgroup == "total_enrollment",          grade_level != "TOTAL", !is.na(grade_level)) %>%   mutate(grade_label = case_when(     grade_level == "PK" ~ "Pre-K",     grade_level == "K" ~ "K",     TRUE ~ paste("Grade", grade_level)   ),   grade_order = case_when(     grade_level == "PK" ~ 0,     grade_level == "K" ~ 1,     TRUE ~ as.numeric(grade_level) + 1   )) %>%   arrange(grade_order) %>%   mutate(grade_label = factor(grade_label, levels = grade_label))  stopifnot(nrow(grade_enr) > 0) grade_enr %>% select(grade_label, n_students) #> # A tibble: 14 × 2 #>    grade_label n_students #>    <fct>            <dbl> #>  1 Pre-K           88063  #>  2 K               86554  #>  3 Grade 01        91396  #>  4 Grade 02        93705  #>  5 Grade 03        94599  #>  6 Grade 04        97367  #>  7 Grade 05        96650  #>  8 Grade 06        97968  #>  9 Grade 07        98315  #> 10 Grade 08        99814  #> 11 Grade 09       102207  #> 12 Grade 10       102068. #> 13 Grade 11       102865  #> 14 Grade 12       105879  ggplot(grade_enr, aes(x = grade_label, y = n_students)) +   geom_col(fill = nj_colors["total"]) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +   labs(title = "NJ's Enrollment Pyramid Shows the Pre-K Boom",        subtitle = "Pre-K (88K) now exceeds Kindergarten (87K) in 2026",        x = "Grade Level", y = "Students") +   theme_nj() +   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

what: 1. A decade of flat spending, then a sharp surge
title: Following the Money: 25 Years of Spending in South Orange-Maplewood
text: 2010s, SOMSD’s actual budgetary cost per pupil barely moved: $14,300 2010 $14,700 2019, real-terms decline account inflation. broke loose. Actuals jumped $14,660 (2020) $18,952 (2024), 2025 budget sets $20,066.
code: pp <- somsd_series("CSG1", "Per Pupil costs", "Actuals") pp_budget_2025 <- somsd_series("CSG1", "Per Pupil costs", "Budgeted") %>%   filter(end_year == 2025)  stopifnot(nrow(pp) > 0, nrow(pp_budget_2025) == 1) pp #> # A tibble: 26 × 3 #>    report_year end_year value #>          <int>    <dbl> <dbl> #>  1        2001     1999  7946 #>  2        2002     2000  8260 #>  3        2003     2001  8691 #>  4        2004     2002  9219 #>  5        2005     2003  9626 #>  6        2006     2004 10271 #>  7        2007     2005 11101 #>  8        2008     2006 11366 #>  9        2009     2007 12594 #> 10        2010     2008 12983 #> # ℹ 16 more rows ggplot(pp, aes(end_year, value)) +   annotate("rect", xmin = 2010, xmax = 2019, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,            fill = "gray85", alpha = 0.5) +   annotate("text", x = 2014.5, y = 19500, label = "flat decade",            color = "gray45", fontface = "italic") +   geom_line(color = somsd_navy, linewidth = 1.2) +   geom_point(color = somsd_navy, size = 2) +   geom_point(data = pp_budget_2025, aes(end_year, value),              color = somsd_orange, size = 3, shape = 17) +   annotate("text", x = 2024.7, y = pp_budget_2025$value + 900,            label = "2025\nbudgeted", color = somsd_orange, size = 3.3) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2025, 5)) +   labs(title = "SOMSD per-pupil spending: flat, then a post-2020 surge",        subtitle = "Budgetary cost per pupil, actuals (2025 is budgeted)",        x = NULL, y = "Cost per pupil",        caption = "Source: NJ DOE Taxpayers' Guide to Educational Spending") +   theme_nj()

title: Following the Money: 25 Years of Spending in South Orange-Maplewood
text: ’s trend drives every contentious school budget meeting: employee benefits share salaries. SOMSD doubled, 11.8% 1999 24.4% 2024. Health-plan premiums (SEHBP) main driver, keep climbing faster pay.
code: benefits <- somsd_series("CSG14", "% of Total Salaries", "Actuals") stopifnot(nrow(benefits) > 0) benefits #> # A tibble: 26 × 3 #>    report_year end_year value #>          <int>    <dbl> <dbl> #>  1        2001     1999 0.118 #>  2        2002     2000 0.13  #>  3        2003     2001 0.142 #>  4        2004     2002 0.142 #>  5        2005     2003 0.164 #>  6        2006     2004 0.186 #>  7        2007     2005 0.195 #>  8        2008     2006 0.214 #>  9        2009     2007 0.211 #> 10        2010     2008 0.214 #> # ℹ 16 more rows ggplot(benefits, aes(end_year, value)) +   geom_area(fill = somsd_orange, alpha = 0.25) +   geom_line(color = somsd_orange, linewidth = 1.2) +   geom_point(color = somsd_orange, size = 2) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),                      limits = c(0, NA)) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2025, 5)) +   labs(title = "Employee benefits as a share of salaries doubled",        subtitle = "SOMSD benefit costs divided by total salaries, 1999-2024",        x = NULL, y = "Benefits / salaries",        caption = "Source: NJ DOE TGES (CSG14)") +   theme_nj()

what: 3. Who pays the bill is shifting
title: Following the Money: 25 Years of Spending in South Orange-Maplewood
text: SOMSD property-tax town: local taxpayers funded 85% budget 2010. state’s share roughly doubled since (12% 26%), local share fell 70%. Federal money never tops percent.
code: revenue <- bind_rows(   somsd_series("VITSTAT_TOTAL", "Revenue: Local %") %>% mutate(source = "Local taxes"),   somsd_series("VITSTAT_TOTAL", "Revenue: State %")  %>% mutate(source = "State aid") ) stopifnot(nrow(revenue) > 0)  revenue %>%   tidyr::pivot_wider(id_cols = end_year, names_from = source, values_from = value) #> # A tibble: 15 × 3 #>    end_year `Local taxes` `State aid` #>       <dbl>         <dbl>       <dbl> #>  1     2010         0.845       0.121 #>  2     2011         0.891       0.086 #>  3     2012         0.857       0.116 #>  4     2013         0.845       0.134 #>  5     2014         0.852       0.124 #>  6     2015         0.861       0.136 #>  7     2016         0.842       0.139 #>  8     2017         0.831       0.148 #>  9     2018         0.825       0.159 #> 10     2019         0.813       0.169 #> 11     2020         0.801       0.183 #> 12     2021         0.761       0.221 #> 13     2022         0.718       0.248 #> 14     2023         0.696       0.244 #> 15     2024         0.704       0.255 ggplot(revenue, aes(end_year, value, color = source)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +   geom_point(size = 2) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("Local taxes" = somsd_navy, "State aid" = somsd_teal)) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2010, 2024, 4)) +   labs(title = "Local taxpayers still carry SOMSD, but state aid is rising",        subtitle = "Share of revenue by source",        x = NULL, y = "Share of revenue", color = NULL,        caption = "Source: NJ DOE TGES Vital Statistics") +   theme_nj()

what: 4. Teacher pay up, class sizes down
title: Following the Money: 25 Years of Spending in South Orange-Maplewood
text: median teacher salary nearly doubled period, $45,000 roughly $84,000. stretch student--teacher ratio drifted 14:1 toward 11:1, district paying teachers, .
code: salary <- somsd_series("CSG16", "Teacher Salary") ratio  <- somsd_series("CSG16", "Student/Teacher ratio") stopifnot(nrow(salary) > 0, nrow(ratio) > 0) salary %>%   inner_join(ratio, by = "end_year", suffix = c("_salary", "_ratio")) %>%   transmute(end_year, median_salary = value_salary, students_per_teacher = value_ratio) #> # A tibble: 26 × 3 #>    end_year median_salary students_per_teacher #>       <dbl>         <dbl>                <dbl> #>  1     2000         48624                 14.2 #>  2     2001         44696                 14   #>  3     2002         50000                 14.2 #>  4     2003         49720                 13.8 #>  5     2004         54897                 13.8 #>  6     2005         58122                 13.4 #>  7     2006         58122                 13.4 #>  8     2007         54369                 12.8 #>  9     2008         67365                 12.8 #> 10     2009         69677                 12.7 #> # ℹ 16 more rows ggplot(salary, aes(end_year, value)) +   geom_line(color = somsd_purple, linewidth = 1.2) +   geom_point(color = somsd_purple, size = 2) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2025, 5)) +   labs(title = "Median teacher salary nearly doubled",        subtitle = "SOMSD median classroom-teacher salary",        x = NULL, y = "Median salary",        caption = "Source: NJ DOE TGES (CSG16)") +   theme_nj() ggplot(ratio, aes(end_year, value)) +   geom_line(color = somsd_teal, linewidth = 1.2) +   geom_point(color = somsd_teal, size = 2) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2025, 5)) +   labs(title = "...while the student-to-teacher ratio fell",        subtitle = "Students per teacher",        x = NULL, y = "Students per teacher",        caption = "Source: NJ DOE TGES (CSG16)") +   theme_nj()

title: Following the Money: 25 Years of Spending in South Orange-Maplewood
text: TGES breaks budgetary cost per pupil components. 2025 budget, classroom instruction far biggest slice (61 cents every dollar), followed support services, plant operations, administration.
code: category_tables <- tribble(   ~category,                  ~table,   "Classroom instruction",    "CSG2",   "Support services",         "CSG6",   "Plant ops & maintenance",  "CSG10",   "Administration",           "CSG8",   "Extracurricular",          "CSG13" )  categories <- map_dfr(seq_len(nrow(category_tables)), function(i) {   d <- reports[["2025"]][[category_tables$table[i]]]   r <- filter(d, district_id == SOMSD, end_year == 2025)   tibble(category = category_tables$category[i],          per_pupil = r[["Per Pupil costs"]][1]) })  stopifnot(nrow(categories) > 0, all(!is.na(categories$per_pupil))) categories %>% arrange(desc(per_pupil)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 2 #>   category                per_pupil #>   <chr>                       <dbl> #> 1 Classroom instruction       12338 #> 2 Support services             3195 #> 3 Plant ops & maintenance      2394 #> 4 Administration               1874 #> 5 Extracurricular               242 ggplot(categories, aes(reorder(category, per_pupil), per_pupil)) +   geom_col(fill = somsd_navy) +   geom_text(aes(label = dollar(per_pupil)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +   coord_flip() +   scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +   labs(title = "Where SOMSD spends, per pupil (2025 budget)",        subtitle = "Classroom instruction dominates the budgetary cost per pupil",        x = NULL, y = "Cost per pupil",        caption = "Source: NJ DOE TGES (CSG2, CSG6, CSG8, CSG10, CSG13)") +   theme_nj()

what: 6. Drifting through the pack of big districts
title: Following the Money: 25 Years of Spending in South Orange-Maplewood
text: TGES ranks district peer group. SOMSD sits large K-12 districts (3,500+ students). ranking, 1 = lowest spender, higher number means spending peers. SOMSD climbed toward top pack 2010, slid near bottom flat decade peers kept growing, recovered mid-pack post-2020 surge.
code: rank <- somsd_series("CSG1", "District rank", "Actuals") stopifnot(nrow(rank) > 0)  # size of the peer group in the most recent guide, for context n_peers <- reports[["2025"]][["CSG1"]] %>%   filter(group == "G. K-12 / 3501 +", end_year == 2025,          !is.na(district_id), district_id != "00NA") %>%   nrow() n_peers #> [1] 100  rank #> # A tibble: 26 × 3 #>    report_year end_year value #>          <int>    <dbl> <int> #>  1        2001     1999    43 #>  2        2002     2000    46 #>  3        2003     2001    51 #>  4        2004     2002    56 #>  5        2005     2003    56 #>  6        2006     2004    59 #>  7        2007     2005    64 #>  8        2008     2006    64 #>  9        2009     2007    72 #> 10        2010     2008    70 #> # ℹ 16 more rows ggplot(rank, aes(end_year, value)) +   geom_line(color = somsd_red, linewidth = 1.2) +   geom_point(color = somsd_red, size = 2) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2024, 4)) +   labs(title = "SOMSD's spending rank among large K-12 districts",        subtitle = paste0("Rank within the ", n_peers,                           "-district peer group (1 = lowest spender)"),        x = NULL, y = "Spending rank",        caption = "Source: NJ DOE TGES (CSG1). Peer-group composition shifts modestly year to year.") +   theme_nj()

what: 7. A closer look at the classroom dollar
title: Following the Money: 25 Years of Spending in South Orange-Maplewood
text: TGES splits classroom instruction three pieces: salaries benefits, supplies textbooks, “purchased services .” Two things jump . Spending books supplies frozen near $200 per pupil 25 years, steep real-terms cut. Meanwhile purchased services, contracted --district instruction, went rounding error ($7 per pupil 1999) $1,804 2024, 15 cents every classroom dollar.
code: classroom_parts <- bind_rows(   somsd_series("CSG3", "Per Pupil costs") %>% mutate(part = "Salaries & benefits"),   somsd_series("CSG4", "Per Pupil costs") %>% mutate(part = "Supplies & textbooks"),   somsd_series("CSG5", "Per Pupil costs") %>% mutate(part = "Purchased services & other") ) %>%   mutate(part = factor(part, levels = c("Salaries & benefits",                                         "Supplies & textbooks",                                         "Purchased services & other"))) stopifnot(nrow(classroom_parts) > 0)  classroom_parts %>%   filter(end_year %in% c(1999, 2008, 2016, 2024)) %>%   select(end_year, part, value) %>%   arrange(end_year, part) #> # A tibble: 12 × 3 #>    end_year part                       value #>       <dbl> <fct>                      <dbl> #>  1     1999 Salaries & benefits         4316 #>  2     1999 Supplies & textbooks         161 #>  3     1999 Purchased services & other     7 #>  4     2008 Salaries & benefits         7065 #>  5     2008 Supplies & textbooks         198 #>  6     2008 Purchased services & other    35 #>  7     2016 Salaries & benefits         7646 #>  8     2016 Supplies & textbooks         195 #>  9     2016 Purchased services & other   736 #> 10     2024 Salaries & benefits         9753 #> 11     2024 Supplies & textbooks         229 #> 12     2024 Purchased services & other  1804 ggplot(classroom_parts, aes(end_year, value, fill = part)) +   geom_area(alpha = 0.9) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("Salaries & benefits" = somsd_navy,                                "Supplies & textbooks" = somsd_teal,                                "Purchased services & other" = somsd_orange)) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2024, 6)) +   labs(title = "The classroom dollar: contracted services moved in",        subtitle = "SOMSD classroom cost per pupil by component",        x = NULL, y = "Cost per pupil", fill = NULL,        caption = "Source: NJ DOE TGES (CSG3, CSG4, CSG5)") +   theme_nj()

what: 8. Facilities: a frozen line on aging buildings
title: Following the Money: 25 Years of Spending in South Orange-Maplewood
text: Plant operations maintenance, cost heating, cleaning, repairing school buildings, barely budged decade. hovered around $1,800 $2,000 per pupil 2008 2022 even total spending climbed, share budget fell 16% 12%. kind deferred upkeep eventually shows capital referendum. (TGES tracks operating cost, separate capital bonds districts float rebuild.)
code: facilities <- somsd_series("CSG10", "Per Pupil costs") %>%   select(end_year, facilities = value) %>%   inner_join(somsd_series("CSG1", "Per Pupil costs") %>% select(end_year, total = value),              by = "end_year") %>%   mutate(share = facilities / total) stopifnot(nrow(facilities) > 0) facilities #> # A tibble: 26 × 4 #>    end_year facilities total share #>       <dbl>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #>  1     1999       1110  7946 0.140 #>  2     2000       1127  8260 0.136 #>  3     2001       1123  8691 0.129 #>  4     2002       1289  9219 0.140 #>  5     2003       1413  9626 0.147 #>  6     2004       1693 10271 0.165 #>  7     2005       1750 11101 0.158 #>  8     2006       1831 11366 0.161 #>  9     2007       1806 12594 0.143 #> 10     2008       2062 12983 0.159 #> # ℹ 16 more rows ggplot(facilities, aes(end_year, share)) +   geom_line(color = somsd_red, linewidth = 1.2) +   geom_point(color = somsd_red, size = 2) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, NA)) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2024, 6)) +   labs(title = "Facilities' slice of the budget shrank",        subtitle = "Plant operations & maintenance as a share of budgetary cost per pupil",        x = NULL, y = "Facilities share of budget",        caption = "Source: NJ DOE TGES (CSG10 / CSG1)") +   theme_nj()

what: 9. What the spending buys: the arts
title: Following the Money: 25 Years of Spending in South Orange-Maplewood
text: TGES tells district spends; School Performance Report tells students actually take. town known arts scene, second question matters. fetch_arts_enrollment() pulls visual performing arts participation, SOMSD runs well ahead state every grade level. gap widest middle school: nearly every 6th-8th grader takes arts course, 86% take music (vs 64% statewide), 30% take dance state rate just 4%.
code: arts_raw <- fetch_arts_enrollment(2024, level = "district") %>%   filter(district_id == "4900") stopifnot(nrow(arts_raw) > 0) arts_any <- arts_raw %>%   transmute(grades,             SOMSD = as.numeric(any_visual_perf_art_district),             `New Jersey` = as.numeric(any_visual_perf_art_state)) %>%   tidyr::pivot_longer(c(SOMSD, `New Jersey`), names_to = "geo", values_to = "pct") stopifnot(nrow(arts_any) > 0) arts_any #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   grades      geo          pct #>   <chr>       <chr>      <dbl> #> 1 Grades 6-8  SOMSD       99.7 #> 2 Grades 6-8  New Jersey  90   #> 3 Grades 9-12 SOMSD       63.4 #> 4 Grades 9-12 New Jersey  51.3 #> 5 Grades KG-5 SOMSD       99.4 #> 6 Grades KG-5 New Jersey  93.6 ggplot(arts_any, aes(grades, pct, fill = geo)) +   geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.75), width = 0.7) +   geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct), "%")),             position = position_dodge(width = 0.75), vjust = -0.4, size = 3.3) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("SOMSD" = somsd_navy, "New Jersey" = "gray65")) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(scale = 1), limits = c(0, 108)) +   labs(title = "SOMSD students take the arts at above-state rates",        subtitle = "Share taking any visual or performing arts course, 2023-24",        x = NULL, y = "Students taking an arts course", fill = NULL,        caption = "Source: NJ DOE School Performance Report (Visual & Performing Arts)") +   theme_nj() arts_disc <- arts_raw %>%   filter(grades == "Grades 6-8") %>%   select(music_district, music_state, dance_district, dance_state,          drama_district, drama_state, vis_arts_district, vis_arts_state) %>%   mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric)) %>%   tidyr::pivot_longer(everything(),                       names_to = c("discipline", "geo"),                       names_pattern = "(.*)_(district|state)",                       values_to = "pct") %>%   mutate(geo = recode(geo, district = "SOMSD", state = "New Jersey"),          discipline = recode(discipline, music = "Music", dance = "Dance",                              drama = "Drama/Theater", vis_arts = "Visual arts")) stopifnot(nrow(arts_disc) > 0) arts_disc #> # A tibble: 8 × 3 #>   discipline    geo          pct #>   <chr>         <chr>      <dbl> #> 1 Music         SOMSD       85.6 #> 2 Music         New Jersey  64.3 #> 3 Dance         SOMSD       29.5 #> 4 Dance         New Jersey   4.3 #> 5 Drama/Theater SOMSD       15.3 #> 6 Drama/Theater New Jersey   7.7 #> 7 Visual arts   SOMSD       78.3 #> 8 Visual arts   New Jersey  68.9 ggplot(arts_disc, aes(reorder(discipline, -pct), pct, fill = geo)) +   geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.75), width = 0.7) +   geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct), "%")),             position = position_dodge(width = 0.75), vjust = -0.4, size = 3.3) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("SOMSD" = somsd_navy, "New Jersey" = "gray65")) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(scale = 1), limits = c(0, 100)) +   labs(title = "Middle-school arts: SOMSD vs New Jersey",        subtitle = "Share of grade 6-8 students taking each art form, 2023-24",        x = NULL, y = "Students participating", fill = NULL,        caption = "Source: NJ DOE School Performance Report (Visual & Performing Arts)") +   theme_nj()

title: Following the Money: 25 Years of Spending in South Orange-Maplewood
text: Every chart comes fetch_tges(). year’s tables (spending_2025 <- fetch_tges(2025)), pulling SOMSD’s benefits trend two lines: Swap district code NJ district, loop fetch_many_tges(2001:2025) full history.

title: SPR Data Dictionary
text: New Jersey Department Education’s School Performance Reports (SPR) databases contain 63 sheets data per year (2017-18 2023-24), covering comprehensive school performance metrics. document provides dictionary available sheets examples use .

title: SPR Data Dictionary
code: # The generic extractor pulls any sheet by its NJ DOE name. # level = "district" returns district + state aggregates from one ~30 MB # workbook; level = "school" returns school-level rows (a much larger download). ca        <- fetch_spr_data("ChronicAbsenteeism", 2024, level = "district") teachers  <- fetch_spr_data("TeachersExperience", 2024, level = "district") grad_dist <- fetch_spr_data("6YrGraduationCohortProfile", 2024, level = "district") # Every sheet comes back as a tidy data frame with cleaned snake_case columns dim(ca) #> [1] 11050    19 head(names(ca), 9) #> [1] "county_id"         "county_name"       "district_id"       #> [4] "district_name"     "subgroup"          "chronic_abs_count" #> [7] "chronic_abs_pct"   "target"            "met_target"

title: SPR Data Dictionary
code: # Convenience wrappers track the sheet renames NJ DOE makes across years, # so you do not have to remember which name a given year uses. ca       <- fetch_chronic_absenteeism(2024, level = "district") ca_grade <- fetch_absenteeism_by_grade(2024, level = "district") days     <- fetch_days_absent(2024, level = "district") # fetch_absenteeism_by_grade() adds a grade_level column (PK, KG, 1-12) sort(unique(ca_grade$grade_level)) #>  [1] "1"  "10" "11" "12" "2"  "3"  "4"  "5"  "6"  "7"  "8"  "9"  "KG" "PK"

title: SPR Data Dictionary
text: districts highest chronic absenteeism, statewide rate moved since COVID? Compare statewide rate pandemic. Wrapping year’s fetch means missing year surfaces warning, silent gap.
code: ca <- fetch_chronic_absenteeism(2024, level = "district") # Districts where more than 30% of students are chronically absent high_absent <- ca %>%   filter(is_district,          subgroup == "total population",          chronically_absent_rate > 30) %>%   arrange(desc(chronically_absent_rate)) %>%   select(district_name, chronically_absent_rate)  stopifnot(nrow(high_absent) > 0) head(high_absent, 8) #> # A tibble: 8 × 2 #>   district_name                                           chronically_absent_r…¹ #>   <chr>                                                                    <dbl> #> 1 LEAD Charter School                                                       71.4 #> 2 Camden Prep, Inc.                                                         50.2 #> 3 Camden City School District                                               46.9 #> 4 KIPP: Cooper Norcross, A New Jersey Nonprofit Corporat…                   45.5 #> 5 Ocean Gate School District                                                43   #> 6 Roseville Community Charter School                                        39.4 #> 7 Seaside Heights School District                                           39.1 #> 8 TEAM Academy Charter School                                               37.7 #> # ℹ abbreviated name: ¹​chronically_absent_rate ca_trend <- map_dfr(c(2019, 2022, 2024), function(yr) {   tryCatch(     fetch_chronic_absenteeism(yr, level = "district") %>%       filter(is_state, subgroup == "total population") %>%       transmute(end_year, chronically_absent_rate),     error = function(e) {       warning("chronic absenteeism ", yr, " failed: ", conditionMessage(e))       NULL     }   ) }) stopifnot(nrow(ca_trend) == 3) ca_trend #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   end_year chronically_absent_rate #>      <dbl>                   <dbl> #> 1     2019                    10.6 #> 2     2022                    18.1 #> 3     2024                    14.9 ggplot(ca_trend, aes(end_year, chronically_absent_rate)) +   geom_line(color = "#c0392b", linewidth = 1) +   geom_point(color = "#c0392b", size = 3) +   geom_text(aes(label = paste0(chronically_absent_rate, "%")), vjust = -1.1) +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2019, 2022, 2024)) +   labs(     title = "NJ chronic absenteeism spiked after COVID and is still elevated",     subtitle = "Statewide share of students chronically absent (total population)",     x = NULL, y = "Chronically absent (%)"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: SPR Data Dictionary
text: 2024 discipline sheet DisciplinaryRemovalsByStudgroup; fetch_disciplinary_removals() picks right sheet name year. grouping column student_group_grade, sheet reports suspension expulsion percentages directly (enrollment denominator).
code: discipline <- fetch_disciplinary_removals(2024, level = "district") # Suspension and expulsion rates by student group, statewide susp_by_group <- discipline %>%   filter(     is_state,     student_group_grade %in% c(       "Statewide", "Black or African American", "Hispanic", "White",       "Students with disabilities", "Economically Disadvantaged Students"     )   ) %>%   transmute(     student_group_grade,     pct_any_suspension = as.numeric(percent_students_any_suspension),     pct_expulsions     = as.numeric(percent_students_expulsions)   ) %>%   arrange(desc(pct_any_suspension))  stopifnot(nrow(susp_by_group) > 0) susp_by_group #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   student_group_grade                 pct_any_suspension pct_expulsions #>   <chr>                                            <dbl>          <dbl> #> 1 Black or African American                         9.02           0.01 #> 2 Students with disabilities                        6.56           0    #> 3 Economically Disadvantaged Students               6.24           0    #> 4 Hispanic                                          4.56           0    #> 5 Statewide                                         4.26           0    #> 6 White                                             2.78           0

title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: 1.4 million students. 580+ districts. 21 counties. 25+ years data New Jersey Department Education, one clean R Python interface. Full documentation – 15 stories interactive charts, getting-started guide, complete function reference.

title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: New Jersey’s charter sector added 8,400+ students 2020 2026, growing 55,600 64,000 - kept growing 2026 even statewide enrollment fell. (source)
code: library(njschooldata) library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) library(scales)  years <- 2020:2026 enr_all <- purrr::map_df(years, ~{   tryCatch(     fetch_enr(.x, tidy = TRUE),     error = function(e) {       warning(paste("Year", .x, "failed:", conditionMessage(e)))       NULL     }   ) })  charter_trend <- enr_all %>%   filter(is_district, subgroup == "total_enrollment", grade_level == "TOTAL") %>%   mutate(sector = ifelse(is_charter, "Charter", "Traditional")) %>%   group_by(end_year, sector) %>%   summarize(n_students = sum(n_students, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")  stopifnot(nrow(charter_trend) > 0) charter_trend #> # A tibble: 14 x 3 #>    end_year sector      n_students #>       <dbl> <chr>            <dbl> #>  1     2020 Charter         55604. #>  2     2020 Traditional   1320225. #>  3     2021 Charter         57480 #>  4     2021 Traditional   1304920 #>  5     2022 Charter         58776. #>  6     2022 Traditional   1302140. #>  7     2023 Charter         58568. #>  8     2023 Traditional   1313352. #>  9     2024 Charter         61295 #> 10     2024 Traditional   1318693 #> 11     2025 Charter         63810. #> 12     2025 Traditional   1317372. #> 13     2026 Charter         64037 #> 14     2026 Traditional   1293412.

title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: White students went 42% 37% NJ public school enrollment since 2020. NJ public schools now decisively majority-minority. (source)
code: # State-level summary aggregated from district totals for time-series consistency state_summary <- enr_all %>%   filter(is_district) %>%   group_by(end_year, subgroup, grade_level) %>%   summarize(n_students = sum(n_students, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")  state_totals <- state_summary %>%   filter(subgroup == "total_enrollment") %>%   select(end_year, grade_level, total = n_students)  state_summary <- state_summary %>%   left_join(state_totals, by = c("end_year", "grade_level")) %>%   mutate(pct = n_students / total)  demo <- state_summary %>%   filter(subgroup %in% c("white", "hispanic", "black", "asian"),          grade_level == "TOTAL") %>%   mutate(subgroup = factor(subgroup, levels = c("white", "hispanic", "black", "asian")))  stopifnot(nrow(demo) > 0) demo %>% select(end_year, subgroup, pct) %>%   mutate(pct = round(pct * 100, 1)) %>%   tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = subgroup, values_from = pct) #> # A tibble: 7 x 5 #>   end_year white hispanic black asian #>      <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1     2020  42       30.3  14.6  10.3 #> 2     2021  40.6     31.1  14.9  10.4 #> 3     2022  39.6     32.1  14.8  10.3 #> 4     2023  38.5     33.2  14.6  10.3 #> 5     2024  37.6     34.1  14.4  10.3 #> 6     2025  36.7     35.0  14.3  10.3 #> 7     2026  36.5     35.2  14.1  10.4

title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: New Jersey lost 9% kindergartners COVID. K enrollment recovered 2024 slipped 2026, Pre-K kept surging past pre-pandemic levels. (source)
code: k_trend <- state_summary %>%   filter(subgroup == "total_enrollment",          grade_level %in% c("PK", "K", "01", "06", "12")) %>%   mutate(grade_label = case_when(     grade_level == "PK" ~ "Pre-K",     grade_level == "K" ~ "Kindergarten",     grade_level == "01" ~ "Grade 1",     grade_level == "06" ~ "Grade 6",     grade_level == "12" ~ "Grade 12",     TRUE ~ grade_level   ))  stopifnot(nrow(k_trend) > 0) k_trend %>%   filter(grade_level %in% c("K", "PK")) %>%   select(end_year, grade_label, n_students) #> # A tibble: 14 x 3 #>    end_year grade_label  n_students #>       <dbl> <chr>             <dbl> #>  1     2020 Kindergarten      90818 #>  2     2020 Pre-K             45013 #>  3     2021 Kindergarten      82604 #>  4     2021 Pre-K             56396 #>  5     2022 Kindergarten      86202 #>  6     2022 Pre-K             65350 #>  7     2023 Kindergarten      85873 #>  8     2023 Pre-K             71615 #>  9     2024 Kindergarten      90783 #> 10     2024 Pre-K             83463 #> 11     2025 Kindergarten      89428 #> 12     2025 Pre-K             87231 #> 13     2026 Kindergarten      86554 #> 14     2026 Pre-K             88063

title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: Full analysis 15 stories: NJ Enrollment Insights – 15 stories covering charter growth, demographics, COVID recovery, poverty gaps, Getting Started – installation, data fetching, tidy format guide Function reference

title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: Source: New Jersey Department Education – data comes directly NJ DOE, federal sources. Available years: Enrollment data 2000-2026. Tidy format (2020+) provides consistent structure district, charter, school-level records. Assessments 2004-2024 span four different testing systems (GEPA, NJASK, PARCC, NJSLA). Suppression rules: NJ DOE suppresses counts 10 data types. Enrollment data uses half-day weighting programs like pre-K, can produce non-integer counts. Census Day: NJ enrollment counts based October 15 enrollment (ASSA reporting). Known caveats: - 2020+ enrollment data includes state-level rows vignette aggregates district-level time-series consistency - Charter schools appear separate “districts” data - Pre-2020 post-2020 data formats differ significantly

title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: New Jersey one largest public school systems country. Enrollment recovered COVID dip 2025 peak, fell 1.7% 2026 - first real decline years. (source)
code: state_total <- state_summary %>%   filter(subgroup == "total_enrollment", grade_level == "TOTAL")  stopifnot(nrow(state_total) > 0) state_total #> # A tibble: 7 x 6 #>   end_year subgroup         grade_level n_students   total   pct #>      <dbl> <chr>            <chr>            <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> #> 1     2020 total_enrollment TOTAL         1375828. 1375828.    1 #> 2     2021 total_enrollment TOTAL         1362400  1362400     1 #> 3     2022 total_enrollment TOTAL         1360916  1360916     1 #> 4     2023 total_enrollment TOTAL         1371921  1371921     1 #> 5     2024 total_enrollment TOTAL         1379988  1379988     1 #> 6     2025 total_enrollment TOTAL         1381182  1381182     1 #> 7     2026 total_enrollment TOTAL         1357450. 1357450.    1

what: 6. The Big Three: Newark, Jersey City, and Paterson
title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: New Jersey’s three largest traditional districts educate 90,000 students combined - nearly 7% state. (source)
code: big_three_names <- c("Newark Public School District",                      "Jersey City Public Schools",                      "Paterson Public School District") big_three_trend <- enr_all %>%   filter(is_district, !is_charter,          district_name %in% big_three_names,          subgroup == "total_enrollment", grade_level == "TOTAL")  stopifnot(nrow(big_three_trend) > 0) big_three_trend %>% select(end_year, district_name, n_students) #> # A tibble: 18 x 3 #>    end_year district_name                   n_students #>       <dbl> <chr>                                <dbl> #>  1     2021 Newark Public School District        40085 #>  2     2021 Jersey City Public Schools           26541 #>  3     2021 Paterson Public School District      25657 #>  4     2022 Newark Public School District        40607 #>  5     2022 Jersey City Public Schools           26890 #>  6     2022 Paterson Public School District      24495 #>  7     2023 Newark Public School District        41430 #>  8     2023 Jersey City Public Schools           26418 #>  9     2023 Paterson Public School District      26067 #> 10     2024 Newark Public School District        42600 #> 11     2024 Jersey City Public Schools           26023 #> 12     2024 Paterson Public School District      24090 #> 13     2025 Newark Public School District        43980 #> 14     2025 Jersey City Public Schools           25692 #> 15     2025 Paterson Public School District      23609 #> 16     2026 Newark Public School District        43216 #> 17     2026 Jersey City Public Schools           25307 #> 18     2026 Paterson Public School District      21849

what: 7. Free/reduced lunch ranges from 98% to under 5%
title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: Urban districts like Passaic City (92%) nearly students free/reduced lunch affluent suburbs like Millburn (2%) almost none - stark measure NJ’s wealth divide. (source)
code: enr_current <- fetch_enr(2026, tidy = TRUE)  frl <- enr_current %>%   filter(is_district, !is_charter,          subgroup == "free_reduced_lunch", grade_level == "TOTAL",          !is.na(pct), n_students >= 100) %>%   arrange(desc(pct)) %>%   head(15) %>%   mutate(district_label = reorder(district_name, pct))  stopifnot(nrow(frl) > 0) frl %>% select(district_name, n_students, pct) #> # A tibble: 15 x 3 #>    district_name                                             n_students   pct #>    <chr>                                                          <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 Kipp: Cooper Norcross, A New Jersey Nonprofit Corporation      2132. 0.98 #>  2 Mastery Schools Of Camden, Inc.                                2788. 0.95 #>  3 Camden Prep, Inc.                                              1425. 0.937 #>  4 Passaic City School District                                  10132. 0.918 #>  5 Lakewood Township School District                              3381. 0.898 #>  6 Woodlynne School District                                       343  0.875 #>  7 Union City School District                                    10409. 0.867 #>  8 Seaside Heights School District                                 127. 0.847 #>  9 Atlantic City School District                                  5104. 0.839 #> 10 New Brunswick School District                                  6929. 0.838 #> 11 Wildwood City School District                                   629. 0.831 #> 12 Elizabeth Public Schools                                      22034. 0.825 #> 13 West New York School District                                  6070. 0.821 #> 14 Long Branch Public School District                             4069. 0.819 #> 15 Bridgeton City School District                                 5013. 0.799

what: 8. English learners approach 45% in some districts
title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: ELL students make nearly 45% Lakewood Plainfield 1% suburban districts - concentration driven immigration patterns. (source)
code: ell <- enr_current %>%   filter(is_district, !is_charter,          subgroup == "lep", grade_level == "TOTAL",          !is.na(pct), n_students >= 50) %>%   arrange(desc(pct)) %>%   head(15) %>%   mutate(district_label = reorder(district_name, pct))  stopifnot(nrow(ell) > 0) ell %>% select(district_name, n_students, pct) #> # A tibble: 15 x 3 #>    district_name                           n_students   pct #>    <chr>                                        <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 Lakewood Township School District            1691. 0.449 #>  2 Plainfield Public School District            4153. 0.436 #>  3 Dover Public School District                 1375. 0.434 #>  4 Irvington Public School District             3315. 0.421 #>  5 Elizabeth Public Schools                    11057. 0.414 #>  6 New Brunswick School District                3365. 0.407 #>  7 Paterson Public School District              8849. 0.405 #>  8 Trenton Public School District               5795. 0.403 #>  9 Red Bank Borough Public School District       453. 0.396 #> 10 Union City School District                   4694. 0.391 #> 11 Perth Amboy Public School District           3820. 0.383 #> 12 Passaic City School District                 4084. 0.37 #> 13 Bridgeton City School District               2309. 0.368 #> 14 Bound Brook School District                   650. 0.343 #> 15 Palisades Park School District                560. 0.331

code: top_10 <- enr_current %>%   filter(is_district, !is_charter,          subgroup == "total_enrollment", grade_level == "TOTAL") %>%   arrange(desc(n_students)) %>%   head(10) %>%   mutate(district_label = reorder(district_name, n_students))  stopifnot(nrow(top_10) > 0) top_10 %>% select(district_name, n_students) #> # A tibble: 10 x 2 #>    district_name                            n_students #>    <chr>                                         <dbl> #>  1 Newark Public School District                43216 #>  2 Elizabeth Public Schools                     26708 #>  3 Jersey City Public Schools                   25307 #>  4 Paterson Public School District              21849 #>  5 Edison Township School District              16191 #>  6 Trenton Public School District               14380. #>  7 Toms River Regional School District          13925 #>  8 Woodbridge Township School District          13425 #>  9 Hamilton Township Public School District     12112 #> 10 Union City School District                   12006

what: 12. Bergen County has more students than several US states
title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: 130,000+ students, Bergen County alone larger public school system entire states like Wyoming, Vermont, North Dakota. (source)
code: county_enr <- enr_current %>%   filter(is_district, subgroup == "total_enrollment", grade_level == "TOTAL") %>%   group_by(county_name) %>%   summarize(n_students = sum(n_students, na.rm = TRUE),             n_districts = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%   filter(county_name != "Charters") %>%   arrange(desc(n_students)) %>%   head(15) %>%   mutate(county_label = reorder(county_name, n_students))  stopifnot(nrow(county_enr) > 0) county_enr #> # A tibble: 15 x 4 #>    county_name n_students n_districts county_label #>    <chr>            <dbl>       <int> <fct> #>  1 Bergen        130172.          76 Bergen #>  2 Essex         125813           23 Essex #>  3 Middlesex     121810.          25 Middlesex #>  4 Union          95696.          23 Union #>  5 Monmouth       87674           51 Monmouth #>  6 Hudson         80614           13 Hudson #>  7 Camden         77633           39 Camden #>  8 Passaic        73151           20 Passaic #>  9 Morris         72082           40 Morris #> 10 Burlington     68970           39 Burlington #> 11 Ocean          63832.          28 Ocean #> 12 Mercer         58960           12 Mercer #> 13 Somerset       48696.          19 Somerset #> 14 Gloucester     46076.          28 Gloucester #> 15 Atlantic       40586           24 Atlantic

dist_sizes <- enr_current %>%   filter(is_district, !is_charter,          subgroup == "total_enrollment", grade_level == "TOTAL")  stopifnot(nrow(dist_sizes) > 0) cat("Districts:", nrow(dist_sizes), "

what: 14. NJ’s enrollment pyramid shows the pre-K boom
title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: 2026 grade-level distribution reveals pre-K surge: PK enrollment (88K) now surpassed Kindergarten (87K), reflecting NJ’s universal pre-K push. (source)
code: grade_enr <- state_summary %>%   filter(end_year == 2026, subgroup == "total_enrollment",          grade_level != "TOTAL", !is.na(grade_level)) %>%   mutate(grade_label = case_when(     grade_level == "PK" ~ "Pre-K",     grade_level == "K" ~ "K",     TRUE ~ paste("Grade", grade_level)   ),   grade_order = case_when(     grade_level == "PK" ~ 0,     grade_level == "K" ~ 1,     TRUE ~ as.numeric(grade_level) + 1   )) %>%   arrange(grade_order) %>%   mutate(grade_label = factor(grade_label, levels = grade_label))  stopifnot(nrow(grade_enr) > 0) grade_enr %>% select(grade_label, n_students) #> # A tibble: 14 x 2 #>    grade_label n_students #>    <fct>            <dbl> #>  1 Pre-K            88063 #>  2 K                86554 #>  3 Grade 01         91396 #>  4 Grade 02         93705 #>  5 Grade 03         94599 #>  6 Grade 04         97367 #>  7 Grade 05         96650 #>  8 Grade 06         97968 #>  9 Grade 07         98315 #> 10 Grade 08         99814 #> 11 Grade 09        102207 #> 12 Grade 10        102068. #> 13 Grade 11        102865 #> 14 Grade 12        105879

what: 15. NJ’s poverty gap: 96 points between districts
title: A Simple Interface for Accessing NJ DOE School Data in R and Python
text: highest-poverty NJ districts 98% students free/reduced lunch. Affluent Westfield 2%. 96-point gap captures NJ’s extreme wealth inequality. (source)
code: frl_all <- enr_current %>%   filter(is_district, !is_charter,          subgroup == "free_reduced_lunch", grade_level == "TOTAL",          !is.na(pct), n_students >= 100) %>%   arrange(desc(pct))  top_5 <- frl_all %>% head(5) %>% mutate(group = "Highest FRL") bottom_5 <- frl_all %>% tail(5) %>% mutate(group = "Lowest FRL") frl_extremes <- bind_rows(top_5, bottom_5) %>%   mutate(district_label = reorder(district_name, pct))  stopifnot(nrow(frl_extremes) > 0) frl_extremes %>% select(district_name, n_students, pct, group) #> # A tibble: 10 x 4 #>    district_name                                             n_students   pct group #>    <chr>                                                          <dbl> <dbl> <chr> #>  1 Kipp: Cooper Norcross, A New Jersey Nonprofit Corporation      2132. 0.98  Highest FRL #>  2 Mastery Schools Of Camden, Inc.                                2788. 0.95  Highest FRL #>  3 Camden Prep, Inc.                                              1425. 0.937 Highest FRL #>  4 Passaic City School District                                  10132. 0.918 Highest FRL #>  5 Lakewood Township School District                              3381. 0.898 Highest FRL #>  6 Pequannock Township School District                             100. 0.05  Lowest FRL #>  7 Scotch Plains-Fanwood School District                           269. 0.047 Lowest FRL #>  8 Bernards Township School District                               131. 0.029 Lowest FRL #>  9 Ridgewood Public School District                                129. 0.024 Lowest FRL #> 10 Livingston Board Of Education School District                   121. 0.019 Lowest FRL

what: Add percentile rank columns for any metric — add_percentile_rank
title: Add percentile rank columns for any metric — add_percentile_rank
text: fundamental building block percentile rank calculations. Given grouped dataframe metric column, calculates percentile rank within group. Percentile rank defined percent comparison entities lesser equal performance. function respects existing grouping dataframe. want calculate percentile rank within specific peer groups (e.g., DFG, county), group data appropriately calling function, use define_peer_group() set grouping. Note: differs simpler percentile_rank(x, xo) util.R calculates percentile rank single value within vector. function operates dataframe columns adds rank/percentile columns.

title: Add percentile rank columns for any metric — add_percentile_rank

text: df dataframe, optionally grouped. Grouping defines comparison set. metric_col Character. column name rank . prefix Character. Optional prefix output column names. NULL, uses metric_col prefix. Default NULL.

title: Add percentile rank columns for any metric — add_percentile_rank

title: Analyze Course Access Equity — analyze_course_access_equity
text: Analyzes equity course access across demographic subgroups multiple years. Calculates access gaps, disparity indices, trends time identify schools large inequities course offerings.

title: Analyze Course Access Equity — analyze_course_access_equity
Analyze Course Access Equity — analyze_course_access_equity
text: df_list named list data frames different years. element named end_year (e.g., list("2022" = df_2022, "2024" = df_2024)). Data frames course enrollment fetch functions. subgroup_cols Character vector specifying subgroup dimensions analyze. NULL (default), attempts auto-detect subgroup columns. Common options: "subgroup" demographic subgroups.

title: Analyze Course Access Equity — analyze_course_access_equity
text: Data frame : year - Year identifier location_id - Combined location identifier subgroup - Demographic subgroup (e.g., "black", "hispanic", "white") access_rate - Course participation rate subgroup total_population_rate - Rate total population access_gap_percentage - Difference total population (percentage points) disparity_index - Ratio subgroup rate total population rate       (1.0 = parity, < 1.0 = -representation, > 1.0 = -representation) trend - Trend classification: "improving", "widening", "stable", "insufficient_data" flag_large_gap - Logical flag gap > 20 percentage points

title: Analyze Course Access Equity — analyze_course_access_equity
text: Equity analysis calculates: Access Rate: Percentage students subgroup enrolled courses Access Gap: Difference subgroup rate total population rate Disparity Index: Ratio subgroup rate total population rate     (1.0 indicates equal access) Trend: Direction change time based linear regression Schools access gaps > 20 percentage points flagged attention.

title: Analyze Course Access Equity — analyze_course_access_equity
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Fetch data for multiple years math_2022 <- fetch_math_course_enrollment(2022) math_2023 <- fetch_math_course_enrollment(2023) math_2024 <- fetch_math_course_enrollment(2024)  # Combine into named list df_list <- list(   "2022" = math_2022,   "2023" = math_2023,   "2024" = math_2024 )  # Analyze equity across all subgroups equity <- analyze_course_access_equity(df_list)  # View schools with large access gaps for Hispanic students equity %>%   dplyr::filter(subgroup == "hispanic", flag_large_gap == TRUE) %>%   dplyr::select(year, school_name, subgroup, access_gap_percentage, disparity_index) } # }

title: Analyze Staff Retention Patterns — analyze_retention_patterns
text: Analyzes staff retention patterns across multiple years demographic subgroups. Calculates retention rates, turnover rates, stability indices trend detection.

title: Analyze Staff Retention Patterns — analyze_retention_patterns
Analyze Staff Retention Patterns — analyze_retention_patterns
text: df_list named list data frames different years. element named end_year (e.g., list("2022" = df_2022, "2024" = df_2024)). Data frames contain staff retention data columns year, location, retention/turnover metrics. by_subgroup Logical; TRUE (default), analyze retention patterns demographic subgroups. FALSE, aggregate across staff.

title: Analyze Staff Retention Patterns — analyze_retention_patterns
text: Data frame : year - Year identifier location_id - Combined location identifier subgroup - Demographic subgroup (by_subgroup = TRUE) retention_rate - Percentage staff returned previous year turnover_rate - Percentage new staff current year stability_index - Composite score combining retention turnover (0-100) trend - Trend classification: "improving", "stable", "declining", "insufficient_data"

title: Analyze Staff Retention Patterns — analyze_retention_patterns
text: stability index calculated : $$stability = (retention_rate + (100 - turnover_rate)) / 2$$ Higher values indicate greater staff stability (retained staff + low turnover). Trend classification uses linear regression multi-year stability scores: improving - Positive slope (stability increasing time) stable - Near-zero slope insufficient data declining - Negative slope (stability decreasing time)

title: Analyze Staff Retention Patterns — analyze_retention_patterns
code: # NOTE: This function requires staff retention data with appropriate columns. # Example assumes you have retention data frames with the required structure. if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Create example data frames with required columns retain_2022 <- data.frame(   year = 2022,   location_id = c("school1", "school2"),   subgroup = c("total", "total"),   retention_rate = c(85.5, 90.2),   turnover_rate = c(14.5, 9.8) )  # Combine into named list df_list <- list(   "2022" = retain_2022 )  # Analyze retention patterns patterns <- analyze_retention_patterns(df_list, by_subgroup = TRUE) } # }

title: Calculate AP/IB Access Rate — calc_ap_access_rate
text: Calculates percentage students access Advanced Placement (AP) International Baccalaureate (IB) courses. Access defined offering least one AP IB course school.

title: Calculate AP/IB Access Rate — calc_ap_access_rate
Calculate AP/IB Access Rate — calc_ap_access_rate
text: df data frame fetch_math_course_enrollment, fetch_science_course_enrollment, course enrollment functions. contain course type student enrollment columns. subgroup Subgroup analyze (default: "total population"). Specify subgroup name (e.g., "black", "hispanic", "economically disadvantaged") calculate access rates demographic group.

title: Calculate AP/IB Access Rate — calc_ap_access_rate
text: Data frame : original columns input data (aggregated one row per school) ap_access_rate - Percentage students AP course access has_ap - Logical indicating school offers AP courses has_ib - Logical indicating school offers IB courses has_both - Logical indicating school offers AP IB vs_state_avg - Difference state average (state data available)

title: Calculate AP/IB Access Rate — calc_ap_access_rate
text: AP/IB access calculated school level (students school access school offers AP/IB courses). access rate represents proportion students schools offer AP/IB courses.

title: Calculate AP/IB Access Rate — calc_ap_access_rate
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Get science course enrollment (includes AP sciences) science <- fetch_science_course_enrollment(2024)  # Calculate AP access rate for total population ap_access <- calc_ap_access_rate(science, subgroup = "total population")  # Calculate AP access rate for economically disadvantaged students ap_access_ed <- calc_ap_access_rate(science,                                     subgroup = "economically disadvantaged")  # View schools with highest AP access ap_access %>%   dplyr::filter(is_school == TRUE) %>%   dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(ap_access_rate)) %>%   dplyr::select(school_name, has_ap, has_ib, ap_access_rate) } # }

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Get disciplinary removals data discipline <- fetch_disciplinary_removals(2024)  # Calculate rates per 1000 students rates <- calc_discipline_rates_by_subgroup(discipline, rate_per = 1000)  # View disproportionality for racial subgroups rates %>%   dplyr::filter(subgroup %in% c("black", "hispanic", "white")) %>%   dplyr::select(school_name, subgroup, discipline_rate, risk_ratio) } # }

title: Calculate Staff Diversity Metrics — calc_staff_diversity_metrics
text: Calculates diversity indices staff demographics using Simpson's Diversity Index. Computes racial gender diversity scores percentile rankings.

title: Calculate Staff Diversity Metrics — calc_staff_diversity_metrics
Calculate Staff Diversity Metrics — calc_staff_diversity_metrics
text: df data frame fetch_staff_demographics. contain staff demographic breakdowns counts. metrics Character vector specifying diversity metrics calculate. Options: "racial" (default), "gender", c("racial", "gender").

title: Calculate Staff Diversity Metrics — calc_staff_diversity_metrics
text: Data frame : location_id - Combined location identifier diversity_index - Overall Simpson's Diversity Index (0-1 scale, higher = diverse) racial_diversity_score - Racial diversity score (0-1 scale) gender_diversity_score - Gender diversity score (0-1 scale) diversity_percentile_rank - Percentile rank vs schools (0-100) diversity_quintile - Quintile rank (1-5, 5 = diverse)

title: Calculate Staff Diversity Metrics — calc_staff_diversity_metrics
text: Simpson's Diversity Index calculated : $$D = 1 - \sum(p_i^2)$$ \(p_i\) proportion staff category \(\). Values range 0 (diversity - staff one category) 1 (maximum diversity - staff evenly distributed across categories).

title: Calculate Staff Diversity Metrics — calc_staff_diversity_metrics
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Get staff demographics data demographics <- fetch_staff_demographics(2024)  # Calculate racial diversity racial_div <- calc_staff_diversity_metrics(demographics, metrics = "racial")  # Calculate both racial and gender diversity all_div <- calc_staff_diversity_metrics(demographics,                                          metrics = c("racial", "gender"))  # View most diverse schools all_div %>%   dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(diversity_index)) %>%   dplyr::select(school_name, diversity_index, diversity_quintile) } # }

title: Calculate STEM Participation Rate — calc_stem_participation_rate
text: Calculates percentage students enrolled STEM courses (Science, Technology, Engineering, Mathematics). Optionally includes excludes Computer Science calculation.

title: Calculate STEM Participation Rate — calc_stem_participation_rate
code: calc_stem_participation_rate(   math_df,   science_df,   cs_df = NULL,   include_cs = TRUE )

title: Calculate STEM Participation Rate — calc_stem_participation_rate
text: math_df data frame fetch_math_course_enrollment math course enrollment data. science_df data frame fetch_science_course_enrollment science course enrollment data. cs_df Optional data frame fetch_cs_enrollment computer science enrollment data. used include_cs = TRUE. include_cs Logical; TRUE (default), includes computer science courses STEM calculation. FALSE, includes math science.

title: Calculate STEM Participation Rate — calc_stem_participation_rate
text: Data frame : school_id - School identifier stem_participation_rate - Percentage students enrolled STEM courses category - Participation category: "low" (<30       "high" (>60 vs_state_avg - Difference state average (state data available) n_stem_students - Total number students STEM courses n_total_students - Total student enrollment

title: Calculate STEM Participation Rate — calc_stem_participation_rate
text: STEM participation rate calculated percentage students enrolled least one STEM course. Students may counted multiple subject areas (e.g., student taking math science), represents unique students data allows, otherwise represents enrollment counts. Category thresholds: Low: < 30 Medium: 30-60 High: > 60

title: Calculate STEM Participation Rate — calc_stem_participation_rate
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Get math and science enrollment math <- fetch_math_course_enrollment(2024) science <- fetch_science_course_enrollment(2024) cs <- fetch_cs_enrollment(2024)  # Calculate STEM participation including CS stem_with_cs <- calc_stem_participation_rate(math, science, cs, include_cs = TRUE)  # Calculate STEM participation excluding CS stem_no_cs <- calc_stem_participation_rate(math, science, include_cs = FALSE)  # View schools with highest STEM participation stem_with_cs %>%   dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(stem_participation_rate)) %>%   dplyr::select(school_name, stem_participation_rate, category, vs_state_avg) } # }

title: Calculate and Analyze Student-Staff Ratios — calc_student_staff_ratio
text: Data frame original columns plus: ratio_type - type ratio calculated student_staff_ratio - Students per staff member (higher = students per staff) ratio_category - "low" (< 10), "medium" (10-20), "high" (> 20) based benchmarks percent_change_vs_state - Percent difference state average (state data available)

title: Calculate and Analyze Student-Staff Ratios — calc_student_staff_ratio
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Get staff ratio data ratios <- fetch_staff_ratios(2024)  # Calculate overall student-staff ratios overall_ratios <- calc_student_staff_ratio(ratios, ratio_type = "overall")  # Calculate teacher-specific ratios teacher_ratios <- calc_student_staff_ratio(ratios, ratio_type = "teachers")  # View schools with highest student-teacher ratios teacher_ratios %>%   dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(student_staff_ratio)) %>%   dplyr::select(school_name, student_staff_ratio, ratio_category) } # }

title: Calculate equity access rate — calculate_access_rate
text: Calculates share students (overall subgroup) attending schools meet exceed performance threshold. replicates MarGrady metric: "share Black students Newark attending school beat state proficiency average."

title: Calculate equity access rate — calculate_access_rate
code: calculate_access_rate(   df_enrollment,   df_performance,   performance_metric = "proficient_above",   threshold_type = c("state_avg", "absolute", "percentile"),   threshold_value = NULL,   enrollment_col = "n_students",   subgroup = NULL,   join_cols = c("end_year", "county_id", "district_id", "school_id") )

title: Calculate equity access rate — calculate_access_rate
text: df_enrollment School-level enrollment data demographic counts df_performance School-level performance data performance_metric Character. Column df_performance use performance measure. Default "proficient_above". threshold_type Character. define "high-performing": "state_avg": state average year/grade/subject "absolute": fixed numeric threshold "percentile": percentile threshold within peers threshold_value Numeric. threshold value. Required "absolute" "percentile" types. "state_avg", ignored. enrollment_col Character. Column df_enrollment containing student counts. Default "n_students". subgroup Character. subgroup calculate access . NULL, calculates total enrollment. Default NULL. join_cols Character vector. Columns join enrollment performance data . Default c("end_year", "county_id", "district_id", "school_id").

title: Calculate equity access rate — calculate_access_rate
text: Dataframe columns: Year entity identifiers n_students_total: Total students subgroup n_students_above: Students -threshold schools pct_access: Percent access high-performing schools

title: Compare Discipline Across Years — compare_discipline_across_years

title: Compare Discipline Across Years — compare_discipline_across_years
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Fetch data for multiple years disc_2022 <- fetch_disciplinary_removals(2022) disc_2023 <- fetch_disciplinary_removals(2023) disc_2024 <- fetch_disciplinary_removals(2024)  # Combine into named list df_list <- list(   "2022" = disc_2022,   "2023" = disc_2023,   "2024" = disc_2024 )  # Compare trends trends <- compare_discipline_across_years(df_list)  # View schools with increasing discipline rates trends %>%   dplyr::filter(metric_name == "discipline_rate", multi_year_trend == "increasing") } # }

title: Fetch 6-Year Graduation Rate data — fetch_6yr_grad_rate

title: Fetch 6-Year Graduation Rate data — fetch_6yr_grad_rate
Fetch 6-Year Graduation Rate data — fetch_6yr_grad_rate
text: end_year school year. Year end academic year - eg 2020-21 school year end_year '2021'. Valid values 2021-2025. level One "school" "district". "school" returns school-level data, "district" returns district state-level data.

title: Fetch 6-Year Graduation Rate data — fetch_6yr_grad_rate
text: dataframe 6-year graduation rates including: end_year, county_id, county_name, district_id, district_name school_id, school_name (school-level data) subgroup - student group (total population, racial/ethnic groups, etc.) grad_rate_6yr - 6-year graduation rate (0-100 scale) continuing_rate - percentage students still enrolled 6 years non_continuing_rate - percentage dropped left persistence_rate - graduates + continuing students (high school persistence) Aggregation flags (is_state, is_district, is_school, is_charter)

title: Fetch 6-Year Graduation Rate data — fetch_6yr_grad_rate
text: 6-year graduation data different source 4-year 5-year data (SPR database vs ACGR files), fetch function rather option fetch_grad_rate().

title: Fetch 6-Year Graduation Rate data — fetch_6yr_grad_rate
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Get 2024 school-level 6-year graduation rates grad6_2024 <- fetch_6yr_grad_rate(2024)  # Get district-level 6-year graduation rates grad6_dist <- fetch_6yr_grad_rate(2024, level = "district") } # }

what: Fetch Chronic Absenteeism Data (Cross-State Standard) — fetch_absence
title: Fetch Chronic Absenteeism Data (Cross-State Standard) — fetch_absence
text: Unified entry point NJ chronic absenteeism data, matching fetch_absence() naming convention used across state packages. Wraps fetch_chronic_absenteeism() SPR database optional tidy normalization.

title: Fetch Chronic Absenteeism Data (Cross-State Standard) — fetch_absence
code: fetch_absence(   end_year,   level = "school",   type = "chronic",   tidy = TRUE,   use_cache = TRUE )

title: Fetch Chronic Absenteeism Data (Cross-State Standard) — fetch_absence
text: end_year school year (2017-2024). Year end academic year — e.g., 2023-24 school year end_year = 2024. level One "school" "district". Default "school" returns school-level data; "district" returns district state-level data. type One "chronic" (default), "by_grade", "days_absent", "essa". Selects underlying absenteeism function call. tidy Logical; TRUE (default), normalizes subgroup names cross-state standards (e.g., "econ_disadv", "lep", "special_ed"). use_cache Logical; TRUE, uses session cache faster repeat calls.

title: Fetch Chronic Absenteeism Data (Cross-State Standard) — fetch_absence
text: data frame chronic absenteeism data. tidy = TRUE,   subgroup names standardized: total — total population white, black, hispanic, asian — race/ethnicity native_american, pacific_islander, multiracial econ_disadv — economically disadvantaged lep — limited English proficiency special_ed — students disabilities male, female

title: Fetch Chronic Absenteeism Data (Cross-State Standard) — fetch_absence
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Get school-level chronic absenteeism with standard subgroup names ca <- fetch_absence(2024)  # District-level data ca_dist <- fetch_absence(2024, level = "district")  # Grade-level breakdown ca_grade <- fetch_absence(2024, type = "by_grade")  # Days absent distribution days <- fetch_absence(2024, type = "days_absent")  # ESSA chronic absenteeism (school-level only) essa <- fetch_absence(2024, type = "essa")  # Without tidy normalization (original NJ subgroup names) ca_raw <- fetch_absence(2024, tidy = FALSE)  # Cross-state filtering patterns library(dplyr) ca %>%   filter(subgroup == "econ_disadv", is_district) %>%   arrange(desc(chronically_absent_rate)) } # }

what: Fetch Chronic Absenteeism Data for Multiple Years — fetch_absence_multi
title: Fetch Chronic Absenteeism Data for Multiple Years — fetch_absence_multi
text: Convenience wrapper calls fetch_absence() year binds results. Skips years fail (e.g., COVID gap 2020-2021) warning.

title: Fetch Chronic Absenteeism Data for Multiple Years — fetch_absence_multi
code: fetch_absence_multi(   end_years,   level = "school",   type = "chronic",   tidy = TRUE,   use_cache = TRUE )

title: Fetch Chronic Absenteeism Data for Multiple Years — fetch_absence_multi
text: end_years Integer vector school years (e.g., 2017:2024). level One "school" "district". type One "chronic", "by_grade", "days_absent", "essa". tidy Logical; TRUE (default), normalizes subgroup names. use_cache Logical; TRUE (default), caches year.

title: Fetch Chronic Absenteeism Data for Multiple Years — fetch_absence_multi
text: data frame years bound together.

title: Fetch Chronic Absenteeism Data for Multiple Years — fetch_absence_multi
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Get all available years of chronic absenteeism ca_all <- fetch_absence_multi(2017:2024)  # COVID gap: 2020-2021 were not reported, will be skipped with a warning ca_all <- fetch_absence_multi(2017:2024)  # Multi-year trend by district library(dplyr) ca_all %>%   filter(subgroup == "total", is_state) %>%   select(end_year, chronically_absent_rate) } # }

title: Fetch ESSA Accountability Status — fetch_essa_status
text: 2024-25 (end_year 2025) SPR redesign reorganized accountability sheets per database file: School DB: keeps ESSAAccountabilityStatus sheet     (one row per identified school). District/State DB: ESSAAccountabilityStatus sheet     removed replaced two new sheets:     ESSAAccountabilityStatusList (one row per identified school,     12-column layout School DB sheet)     ESSAAccountabilityStatusCounts (per-district CSI/ATSI/TSI tallies).     List sheet structural analogue legacy     ESSAAccountabilityStatus sheet – carries per-entity status     category_of_identification downstream functions     identify_focus_schools require – function maps     district-level 2025+ requests ESSAAccountabilityStatusList.     Counts sheet holds aggregate tallies used .

title: Fetch ESSA Accountability Status — fetch_essa_status
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # School-level status (all years use the ESSAAccountabilityStatus sheet) essa <- fetch_essa_status(2024)  # District/State-level status for the redesigned 2024-25 database essa_dist <- fetch_essa_status(2025, level = "district")  # Identify schools needing comprehensive support library(dplyr) fetch_essa_status(2025) %>%   filter(grepl("Comprehensive", status_for_sy)) } # }

title: Fetch Postsecondary Enrollment Rates — fetch_postsecondary

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Get all postsecondary enrollment data postsec <- fetch_postsecondary()  # Filter for 16-month rates only postsec_16mo <- postsec[postsec$measurement_type == "16_month", ]  # Filter for district-level data only district_rates <- postsec[postsec$is_district, ] } # }

what: Fetch Student Growth Percentile (SGP) Data — fetch_sgp
title: Fetch Student Growth Percentile (SGP) Data — fetch_sgp
text: Downloads NJ Student Growth Percentile (median SGP / mSGP) data redesigned 2024-25 School Performance Reports databases. SGP measures much students grew academically relative peers similar score histories; median SGP (mSGP) summarizes school's district's growth.

title: Fetch Student Growth Percentile (SGP) Data — fetch_sgp
Fetch Student Growth Percentile (SGP) Data — fetch_sgp
text: end_year school year. Supported years depend type; see Supported years . level One "school" "district". "school" returns school-level data; "district" returns district state-level data. type One "trends" (default), "by_grade", "by_performance_level". Selects SGP sheet fetch.

title: Fetch Student Growth Percentile (SGP) Data — fetch_sgp
text: Data frame median SGP data. Columns vary type: types: end_year, county_id, county_name, district_id,       district_name, school_id, school_name, school_year, plus aggregation       flags (is_state, is_county, is_district, is_school, is_charter, ...). type = "trends": subgroup, ELA Math       entity median (ela_median_sgp, math_median_sgp)       *_category growth label, plus statewide comparison       (ela_median_sgp_state, math_median_sgp_state)       category. level = "school" entity median school       value; level = "district" district value. Pre-2025       years additionally carry ela_met_target/math_met_target       NA *_category columns. 2025 sheet       multi-year trend filtered requested year adds       school_year column; legacy sheets single-year. type = "by_grade": subject, grade,       median_sgp, median_sgp_category. type = "by_performance_level": subject,       njsla_performance_level, median_sgp,       median_sgp_category.

title: Fetch Student Growth Percentile (SGP) Data — fetch_sgp
text: type argument selects one three SPR sheets: "trends" (default) – StudentGrowthTrends (legacy     StudentGrowth): median SGP broken student group, ELA     Math. One row per entity per student group. Pre-2025 years carry     legacy MetTarget flag ela_met_target/math_met_target     NA *_category (growth-category labels new 2025). "by_grade" – StudentGrowthbyGrade (legacy     StudentGrowthByGrade): median SGP subject (ELA/Math) grade     (Grades 4-8). growth category reported 2023; earlier     years return NA median_sgp_category. "by_performance_level" –     StudentGrowthByPerformLevel: median SGP subject prior-year     NJSLA performance level (Levels 1-5). 2017-2019 sheet reports     different statistic (growth-band percentage distribution)     supported. Median SGP value columns returned numeric; suppressed cells (“Fewer 10 testers”) become NA, suppression reason preserved companion *_category column. Supported years (vary type): trends: 2018, 2019, 2023, 2024, 2025. by_grade: 2018, 2019, 2023, 2024, 2025. by_performance_level: 2023, 2024, 2025. SY2019-20 SY2021-22 (end_year 2020-2022) unavailable every type – NJ produced Student Growth Percentiles COVID statewide-assessment pause.

title: Fetch Student Growth Percentile (SGP) Data — fetch_sgp
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # School-level median SGP by student group (default type = "trends") sgp <- fetch_sgp(2025)  # District/state-level growth trends sgp_dist <- fetch_sgp(2025, level = "district")  # Median SGP by grade sgp_grade <- fetch_sgp(2025, type = "by_grade")  # Median SGP by NJSLA performance level sgp_perf <- fetch_sgp(2025, type = "by_performance_level")  # Compare a district's ELA growth to the statewide median library(dplyr) fetch_sgp(2025, level = "district") %>%   filter(is_district, subgroup == "total population") %>%   select(district_name, ela_median_sgp, ela_median_sgp_state) } # }
